questions.q: In Article 1, the Constitution describes India as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Article 1(1) declares that 'India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States.'

questions.q: Indian federalism divides powers primarily between which two levels of government?
questions.explanation: The federal scheme distributes legislative and administrative powers between the Union and the States.

questions.q: The division of legislative powers in Indian federalism is set out in which Schedule of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Seventh Schedule contains the Union, State and Concurrent Lists that distribute legislative subjects.

questions.q: Indian federalism is often described as having which additional character?
questions.explanation: India follows a federal structure while retaining strong unitary features, especially in emergencies.

questions.q: Which part of the Constitution chiefly deals with relations between the Union and the States?
questions.explanation: Part XI of the Constitution governs legislative and administrative relations between the Union and the States.

questions.q: Which of the following is a feature commonly associated with a federal system?
options: An unwritten code of customs only
A directly elected head of state
questions.explanation: Federal systems typically have a written constitution that divides powers between two levels of government.

questions.q: Under the Constitution, the boundaries and names of states are listed in which Schedule?
questions.explanation: The First Schedule lists the States and Union Territories of India and their territories.

questions.q: In Indian federalism, residuary powers of legislation are vested in which level?
questions.explanation: Article 248 vests residuary powers, those not in any list, in the Union Parliament.

questions.q: Which body recommends the sharing of tax revenues between the Union and the States?
questions.explanation: The Finance Commission, under Article 280, recommends the distribution of tax revenues between the Union and the States.

questions.q: Each state in the Indian federal structure has a constitutional head known as the:
questions.explanation: The Governor is the constitutional head of a state, appointed by the President.

questions.q: Which constitutional body was established by the 73rd Amendment to strengthen the local tier of governance?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment of 1992 gave constitutional status to rural Panchayati Raj institutions.

questions.q: The Inter-State Council, a body to promote Centre-State coordination, is provided for under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 263 empowers the President to establish an Inter-State Council for coordination between governments.

questions.q: Which Article allows Parliament to legislate on a State List subject in the national interest if the Rajya Sabha so resolves?
questions.explanation: Article 249 lets Parliament legislate on a State List matter if the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by two-thirds majority.

questions.q: When a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, Parliament may legislate on which subjects?
options: Subjects in the State List as well
questions.explanation: Under Article 250, during a National Emergency Parliament can make laws even on State List subjects.

questions.q: The Commission whose recommendations led to states being reorganised largely on linguistic lines in 1956 was the:
questions.explanation: The States Reorganisation Commission's report led to the States Reorganisation Act of 1956.

questions.q: The 1980s commission that examined Centre-State relations in detail was the:
questions.explanation: The Sarkaria Commission, set up in 1983, reviewed and recommended reforms in Centre-State relations.

questions.q: Which institution, established in 2015, describes 'cooperative federalism' as one of its guiding aims?
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog, the successor to the Planning Commission, was set up in 2015 to foster cooperative federalism.

questions.q: Zonal Councils, created to promote inter-state cooperation, were established under which legislation?
options: The Representation of the People Act, 1951
questions.explanation: The States Reorganisation Act of 1956 provided for the creation of Zonal Councils as advisory bodies.

questions.q: In matters administered jointly, which list contains subjects on which both the Union and States may legislate?
questions.explanation: The Concurrent List contains subjects on which both Parliament and State Legislatures may make laws.

questions.q: The GST Council, a federal body for indirect tax decisions, was created by which Constitutional Amendment?
questions.explanation: The 101st Amendment of 2016 introduced the Goods and Services Tax and created the GST Council under Article 279A.

questions.q: Which Article provides for the President's rule in a state when its constitutional machinery fails?
questions.explanation: Article 356 allows the imposition of President's rule in a state on the failure of constitutional machinery.

questions.q: All-India Services common to the Union and the States are provided for under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 312 provides for the creation of All-India Services common to the Union and the States.

questions.q: The 1994 Supreme Court judgment that laid down guidelines on the use of Article 356 and called federalism part of the basic structure was:
options: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
questions.explanation: In S.R. Bommai (1994), the Supreme Court held federalism to be part of the basic structure and restrained misuse of Article 356.

questions.q: The second commission on Centre-State relations, which submitted its report in 2010, was chaired by:
questions.explanation: The Punchhi Commission, headed by Justice M.M. Punchhi, submitted its report on Centre-State relations in 2010.

questions.q: Under Article 254, in case of repugnancy on a Concurrent List subject, which law generally prevails?
options: The older of the two laws
questions.explanation: Article 254 provides that, in case of conflict on a Concurrent subject, Union law generally prevails over State law.

questions.q: The administrative framework of Indian federalism drew heavily from which earlier statute?
questions.explanation: The federal scheme, including the division of powers, was largely modelled on the Government of India Act, 1935.

questions.q: Which feature is often cited as evidence that Indian federalism leans toward a strong Centre?
options: Equal representation of all states in the Rajya Sabha
A single, unified citizenship for the whole country
An absolute right of states to secede
questions.explanation: India provides a single citizenship for the whole country, a unitary feature within its federal structure.

questions.q: Asymmetric federal arrangements, where some units have special provisions, are illustrated in India by:
options: Identical powers granted to every state
A uniform tax rate fixed for all states
A single legislature for all states
Special provisions for certain states under Articles 371 to 371-J
questions.explanation: Articles 371 to 371-J contain special provisions for certain states, reflecting asymmetric federalism.

questions.q: The power to alter the boundaries, area or name of an existing state rests with which body?
options: Parliament, by ordinary law under Article 3
questions.explanation: Article 3 empowers Parliament to form new states or alter areas, boundaries or names of existing states.

questions.q: During a Financial Emergency under Article 360, the Union may give directions to the states regarding:
options: Choice of the state Chief Minister
questions.explanation: Under Article 360, the Union may direct states to observe specified canons of financial propriety during a Financial Emergency.
